review_summary: The transcript is sufficient for compact questions on clip-based training and test-time averaging over multiple video clips, so a separate clip is not needed.

question_bank.question: Why do practical video classifiers usually train on short, low-resolution clips instead of full raw videos?
choices: Because full raw videos are too large for memory and compute, so clip training makes learning feasible
Because long videos do not contain useful temporal information
Because classification losses only work on inputs with exactly 16 frames
Because lower resolution always improves recognition accuracy
question_bank.explanation: Full raw videos create huge input, activation, and storage costs. Training on shorter clips with reduced resolution and frame rate keeps the problem tractable while still exposing the model to meaningful motion patterns.

question_bank.question: A model is trained on short clips from a long video. What is a common test-time strategy for producing one prediction for the whole video?
choices: Sample several clips and average their predictions
Use only the very first frame of the video
Concatenate every frame into one giant input tensor
Predict each clip independently and ignore the rest
question_bank.explanation: When a full video is too long to process at once, a common approach is to evaluate several clips and combine them. Averaging clip-level predictions gives a practical whole-video estimate.
